Ref: {{ref}}
Ownership Culture Workshop Guide
Facilitator Resource — Quarterly Employee Engagement
Company
{{companyName}}
Workshop Quarter
{{workshopQuarter}}
Prepared For
{{recipientName}}
Date
{{sentDate}}
Purpose: This guide provides a structured framework for running quarterly ownership mindset workshops. Regular workshops build employee understanding of the ESOP, strengthen the ownership culture, and identify concerns early.
§ 01Workshop Overview
Quarterly (4 per year)
90 minutes
All employees (or department-level groups of 15–30)
Designated internal champion or senior leader
Conference room, large break room, or video call

The workshop is interactive, not a lecture. The facilitator guides discussion rather than delivering a presentation. Employees should leave feeling more connected to the company’s mission and more confident about how the ESOP works.

§ 02Preparation Checklist

Complete these items at least one week before the workshop.

Room or video link reserved — quiet space with whiteboard or screen
Materials printed — this facilitator guide, activity handouts, pens and markers
Pre-survey distributed — short 3–5 question survey to gauge current sentiment
Pre-read completed — review last quarter’s Employee Feedback Tracker for themes
ESOP education materials ready — current stock value, account growth examples, distribution timeline
Attendance confirmed — calendar invite sent, manager reminders, coverage for shift workers
§ 03Workshop Agenda

90-minute timed breakdown. Adjust timings as needed but keep the total under 90 minutes.

1.Welcome & Icebreaker (10 min). Open with a brief check-in: “In one word, how are you feeling about being an owner today?” Capture responses on a whiteboard. This sets the tone and surfaces immediate concerns.
2.ESOP Education Module (20 min). Cover one focused topic per quarter (see Discussion Topics below). Use simple language, real numbers from the company, and avoid jargon. Include a brief Q&A.
3.Group Discussion (30 min). Break into small groups of 4–6. Give each group a discussion question related to the quarterly theme. Each group reports back one key insight. Facilitator captures themes on a whiteboard.
4.Ownership Activity (20 min). Run one activity from the Activities section below. Choose the activity that best fits the quarterly theme and group size.
5.Wrap-Up & Action Items (10 min). Summarize key takeaways. Announce any actions the company will take based on today’s discussion. Remind employees of resources (ESOP booklet, HR contacts, trustee). Collect quick exit feedback.
§ 04Discussion Topics

Use one topic per quarter. Rotate through these themes annually, adding company-specific examples each time.

Q1.“What does ownership mean to you?”
Education focus: Introduction to the ESOP — what it is, how accounts grow, how company performance connects to personal wealth. Reference the “Welcome to Your ESOP” booklet.
Q2.“How does our work create value?”
Education focus: Business fundamentals — how revenue, profit, and share value connect. Explain EBITDA in plain language. Show how efficiency improvements increase stock price for everyone.
Q3.“Decision-making like an owner.”
Education focus: Day-to-day ownership mindset — waste reduction, customer retention, quality. Discuss real scenarios: “Would you make a different choice if you owned the company?” (They do.)
Q4.“Celebrating wins and learning from setbacks.”
Education focus: Year-in-review — stock value change, company milestones, employee contributions. Discuss repurchase obligation in accessible terms. Preview next year’s goals.
§ 05Ownership Activities

Choose one activity per workshop. Each is designed for groups of 15–30 and takes approximately 20 minutes.

A.“Cost of Waste” Exercise. Present a real (anonymized) example of waste: a returned shipment, reworked order, overtime cost. Ask: “What would an owner do differently?” Calculate the annual impact and translate to per-employee share value.
B.“Customer Impact” Mapping. Pick one customer or contract. Map the chain: who sells, delivers, supports, invoices. Each person identifies how their role affects retention. Discuss: “If we lose this customer, what happens to our stock?”
C.“If I Owned This” Scenario. Present a hypothetical business decision: invest in new equipment, hire for a new role, or change a process? Groups debate and vote. Facilitator reveals what the company actually did and why.
D.Peer Recognition Round. Each employee writes one anonymous note recognizing a colleague who “acted like an owner” this quarter. Notes are collected and read aloud. Best used in Q4 as a year-end wrap-up.
§ 06Facilitator Notes

Tips for running an effective workshop.

A.Handling skepticism. Acknowledge it directly: “That’s a fair concern. Let’s talk about it.” Use data and specific examples rather than cheerleading. If you don’t know the answer, say so and follow up.
B.Managing dominant voices. Use small group breakouts to ensure quieter employees contribute. Set ground rules: “Everyone shares one idea before anyone shares a second.”
C.Keeping it constructive. If discussion turns negative, redirect: “What would make this better?” Focus on what employees can influence, not what they can’t.
D.Capturing feedback. Assign someone to take notes. Record themes, not names. Log all concerns and questions in the Employee Feedback Tracker for the next Forhemit stewardship review.
E.Avoiding legal pitfalls. Do not promise specific stock values, returns, or timelines. Say “the value depends on company performance” not “your account will be worth $X.” Refer tax or legal questions to the appropriate professional.
§ 07Post-Workshop Actions

After each workshop, complete these follow-up steps within one week.

1.Distribute a brief feedback survey. 3–5 questions: was the workshop valuable, what topic should we cover next, any unanswered questions?
2.Follow up on action items. Any commitments made during the workshop should be tracked and communicated back to employees within two weeks.
3.Log feedback in the Employee Feedback Tracker. Record questions, concerns, and positive feedback for the next quarterly Forhemit stewardship review.
4.Report to Forhemit stewardship lead. Share a summary of themes, sentiment, and any escalated items with your Forhemit contact.
Reminder: The Employee Feedback Tracker template is available at internal/06-post-close/employee-feedback-tracker.html. Use it to consolidate feedback across all channels before each quarterly review.

CONFIDENTIAL
For company leadership use. Do not distribute to employees.
